rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes when Procurement, Engineering, and Production provide conflicting cost assumptions
rows.plain: The person responsible for the purchase wants to worry less when the buying, technical, and production teams give different estimates of the cost. This matters because a reliable budget is needed before committing to the purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes over conflicting cost assumptions is a financial-emotional error unrelated to control. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of feeling exposed after an unanswered lifecycle cost question arises during the commitment decision
rows.plain: Spend less time feeling vulnerable when someone asks an unanswered question about the full cost of owning and using the purchase. This helps the buyer make the commitment with a clear understanding of the financial impact.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed after an unanswered lifecycle-cost question is a personal-defensibility error unrelated to control. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether authorization will precede qualification and production-start milestones
rows.plain: Know sooner whether approval will come before testing and production-start deadlines. This helps prevent the start of work from being delayed.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty whether authorization precedes milestones is a funding/schedule delay concern, peripheral to control. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt caused by conflicting financial and milestone-protection evidence
rows.plain: The buyer needs clear proof that the money and key project deadlines are protected. Conflicting financial or progress information makes it difficult to know whether the project is on track.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting financial/milestone evidence is a decision-support ambiguity unrelated to control. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while awaiting authorization-critical information from stakeholders
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries while waiting for information from other people that is needed to approve the purchase. This helps keep important deadlines and approvals on track.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes awaiting authorization-critical information is an emotional-latency error unrelated to control. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of alarm as the authorization decision window narrows without resolution
rows.plain: Reduce worry when the time left to approve a decision is getting shorter and no decision has been reached. This helps prevent a rushed or missed project milestone.
rows.imp_rat: Alarm as authorization window narrows is a funding-emotional error unrelated to control. Held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to defend the absence of validation gaps during inspection questioning
rows.plain: The person wants to show that there are no missing tests or proof needed to confirm that the process works as intended. This matters because an inspection may expose any unproven part of the process.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed defending absence of validation gaps is a personal-defensibility error, indirect to physical control. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of doubt caused by unresolved compliance-scope exclusions at authorization
rows.plain: Spend less time worrying that some required checks or rules were left out when the purchase was approved. This helps the buyer know the purchase will not later be challenged for missing coverage.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from unresolved compliance-scope exclusions is a contracting ambiguity, indirect to physical control. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while awaiting confirmation that the funded scope contains no validation gap
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries while waiting for proof that the paid-for work includes all the testing needed to show the process works properly. This helps them avoid problems during a later inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes awaiting confirmation of no validation gap is an emotional-latency error, tangential to control. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that ownership or Finance blames the purchase executor for authorizing scope beyond approved capital limits
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that company owners or the money team blame the purchasing worker for approving more work than the agreed investment budget allowed. Purchases and changes need to stay within the approved scope and limit.
rows.imp_rat: Blame for authorizing scope beyond capital limits is a financial-reputational error unrelated to control. Held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost when the authorized investment is judged unfit for sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust when an approved purchase turns out not to be suitable for making sterile products and filling them into their final containers. The investment should meet the needs of this work before it is approved.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when investment judged unfit for fill-finish is a reputational error, indirect to physical control. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of reputational damage attributed to avoidable lifecycle expense from the authorized investment
rows.plain: Make sure the approved investment does not create avoidable costs over its entire useful life. Unnecessary long-term spending can harm the person's reputation.
rows.imp_rat: Reputational damage from avoidable lifecycle expense is a cost/reputation error unrelated to the transfer job. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which ownership or Finance bypasses the purchase executor in subsequent capital decisions because of perceived weak stewardship
rows.plain: Owners or finance leaders should go around the person responsible for purchases less often in later investment decisions because they doubt how well that person managed earlier money. This helps preserve trust in future spending decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Executor bypassed for perceived weak stewardship is a governance-reputational error unrelated to control. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inspection or validation exposure from funded scope is attributed to the purchase executor's authorization
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that reviewers blame the person who approved the purchase for problems or missing proof connected to the funded work. This helps show that the approval was responsible.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection exposure attributed to executor authorization is a blame/reputational error, indirect to physical control. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the purchase executor is bypassed in future sterile fill-finish investment decisions because the authorization is viewed as under-scoped
rows.plain: The purchasing worker should be included more often in future investment decisions about equipment and processes for making sterile products, rather than being left out because the approval was seen as too limited. This matters because early involvement can help make sure the investment covers what is truly needed.
rows.imp_rat: Executor bypassed for under-scoped authorization is a career-reputational error remote from control execution. Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that capital-governance stakeholders blame the purchase executor for follow-on compliance spending caused by omissions in the authorized scope
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that people overseeing spending blame the buyer for later compliance costs caused by missing items in the approved purchase. This matters because omissions could create unexpected costs and unfair criticism.
rows.imp_rat: Blaming the executor for follow-on compliance spending is a reputational/waste error remote from physical control. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which Quality, Engineering, or Finance second-guesses the purchase executor's compliance judgment during funding approval
rows.plain: Quality, engineering, and finance staff should trust the buyer’s judgment that a purchase follows the required rules when approving its funding. This helps the purchase move forward without repeated challenges.
rows.imp_rat: Cross-functional second-guessing of compliance judgment is a governance-reputational error unrelated to control. Ceiling 1.0.
